chunks.text:
About the Association of British Insurers

The Association of British Insurers is the voice of the UK’s world-leading insurance and long-term savings industry. A productive and inclusive sector, our industry supports towns and cities across Britain in building back a balanced and innovative economy, employing over 310,000 individuals in high-skilled, lifelong careers, two-thirds of which are outside of London.

Our members manage investments of nearly £1.7 trillion, collect and pay over £16 billion in taxes to the Government and support communities across the UK by enabling trade, risk-taking, investment and innovation.

We are also a global success story, the largest in Europe and the fourth largest in the world.

The ABI represents over 200 member companies, including most household names and specialist providers, giving peace of mind to customers across the UK.

**Summary**

Overall, we are supportive of the Bill, but want to ensure that the provisions relating to online financial scams promoted via paid-for advertisements do not leave gaps for fraudsters to exploit.

Consumers’ confidence is being eroded by the ongoing proliferation of online financial scams, including those predicated on impersonation of financial services providers, and facilitated through online advertising. Both the insurance and long-term savings sectors are impacted by financial scams perpetrated via online paid-for advertisements, which can deprive vulnerable consumers of their life savings and leave deep emotional scars.
chunks.display_text:
About the Association of British Insurers

The Association of British Insurers is the voice of the UK’s world-leading insurance and long-term savings industry. A productive and inclusive sector, our industry supports towns and cities across Britain in building back a balanced and innovative economy, employing over 310,000 individuals in high-skilled, lifelong careers, two-thirds of which are outside of London.

Our members manage investments of nearly £1.7 trillion, collect and pay over £16 billion in taxes to the Government and support communities across the UK by enabling trade, risk-taking, investment and innovation.

We are also a global success story, the largest in Europe and the fourth largest in the world.

The ABI represents over 200 member companies, including most household names and specialist providers, giving peace of mind to customers across the UK.

**Summary**

Overall, we are supportive of the Bill, but want to ensure that the provisions relating to online financial scams promoted via paid-for advertisements do not leave gaps for fraudsters to exploit.

Consumers’ confidence is being eroded by the ongoing proliferation of online financial scams, including those predicated on impersonation of financial services providers, and facilitated through online advertising. Both the insurance and long-term savings sectors are impacted by financial scams perpetrated via online paid-for advertisements, which can deprive vulnerable consumers of their life savings and leave deep emotional scars.

chunks.text: proliferation of online financial scams, including those predicated on impersonation of financial services providers, and facilitated through online advertising. Both the insurance and long-term savings sectors are impacted by financial scams perpetrated via online paid-for advertisements, which can deprive vulnerable consumers of their life savings and leave deep emotional scars. While the inclusion of User Generated Content was a good start, the vast majority of online scams are perpetrated via paid-for advertisements. It is crucial that paid-for ads are included in the scope of the Bill, and the ABI wholeheartedly welcomed the announcement confirming this will be the case. This shows that the Government has listened to the many different sectors and organisations, including our own, which have called for paid-for fraudulent adverts to be stopped on social media platforms and search engines. The changes are a huge step towards making the UK the "safest place in the world to be online". We believe it is imperative that these proposals are implemented without any gaps or loopholes. **Paid-for digital advertising**
chunks.display_text: proliferation of online financial scams, including those predicated on impersonation of financial services providers, and facilitated through online advertising. Both the insurance and long-term savings sectors are impacted by financial scams perpetrated via online paid-for advertisements, which can deprive vulnerable consumers of their life savings and leave deep emotional scars. While the inclusion of User Generated Content was a good start, the vast majority of online scams are perpetrated via paid-for advertisements. It is crucial that paid-for ads are included in the scope of the Bill, and the ABI wholeheartedly welcomed the announcement confirming this will be the case. This shows that the Government has listened to the many different sectors and organisations, including our own, which have called for paid-for fraudulent adverts to be stopped on social media platforms and search engines. The changes are a huge step towards making the UK the "safest place in the world to be online". We believe it is imperative that these proposals are implemented without any gaps or loopholes. **Paid-for digital advertising**

chunks.text: paid-for fraudulent adverts to be stopped on social media platforms and search engines. The changes are a huge step towards making the UK the "safest place in the world to be online". We believe it is imperative that these proposals are implemented without any gaps or loopholes. **Paid-for digital advertising** Between September 2019-September 2020, as the nation adjusted to an increasingly online way of life, Action Fraud received more than **17,000 reports of investment fraud**, amounting to **£657.4m in reported losses** – a 28% increase on the same period the previous year. In 2020, Action Fraud reported that £78m was lost to brand cloning scams, amounting to an average and potentially life-changing loss of over £45,000 per victim \[1\]. One insurer had over 1284 bond scams reported to it, of which 174 cases suffered a financial loss (of over £7m). One important step in protecting consumers against these catastrophic losses has been the inclusion of fraud and financial crime as priority offences in the Bill, meaning that tech firms must be proactive and act more quickly to remove illegal content, and that Ofcom can take faster enforcement action against those that fail to comply. These duties must, however, apply equally to search engines and social media platforms. In order to achieve the Bill’s central objective of making the UK "the safest place in the world to be online", then **Government must apply the measures in the Bill to paid-for advertisements**. As the Draft Bill Committee’s report makes clear: *excluding paid-for advertising will leave service providers with little incentive to remove harmful adverts, and risks encouraging further proliferation of such content.*
chunks.display_text: paid-for fraudulent adverts to be stopped on social media platforms and search engines. The changes are a huge step towards making the UK the "safest place in the world to be online". We believe it is imperative that these proposals are implemented without any gaps or loopholes. **Paid-for digital advertising** Between September 2019-September 2020, as the nation adjusted to an increasingly online way of life, Action Fraud received more than **17,000 reports of investment fraud**, amounting to **£657.4m in reported losses** – a 28% increase on the same period the previous year. In 2020, Action Fraud reported that £78m was lost to brand cloning scams, amounting to an average and potentially life-changing loss of over £45,000 per victim \[1\]. One insurer had over 1284 bond scams reported to it, of which 174 cases suffered a financial loss (of over £7m). One important step in protecting consumers against these catastrophic losses has been the inclusion of fraud and financial crime as priority offences in the Bill, meaning that tech firms must be proactive and act more quickly to remove illegal content, and that Ofcom can take faster enforcement action against those that fail to comply. These duties must, however, apply equally to search engines and social media platforms. In order to achieve the Bill’s central objective of making the UK "the safest place in the world to be online", then **Government must apply the measures in the Bill to paid-for advertisements**. As the Draft Bill Committee’s report makes clear: *excluding paid-for advertising will leave service providers with little incentive to remove harmful adverts, and risks encouraging further proliferation of such content.*

chunks.text: safest place in the world to be online", then **Government must apply the measures in the Bill to paid-for advertisements**. As the Draft Bill Committee’s report makes clear: *excluding paid-for advertising will leave service providers with little incentive to remove harmful adverts, and risks encouraging further proliferation of such content.* The announcement earlier this year signalling this change was welcomed across Parliament and industry. However, we have concerns about potential gaps in the Bill’s provisions for tackling scams which criminals will seek to exploit. 1.**The legal requirement for search engines to tackle scam adverts is weaker and seems less onerous than for social media platforms.** · The Bill stipulates that firms defined as ‘Category 1’ - which are **large user-to-user sites such as Twitter and Facebook** - will have a duty to prevent people from encountering scam content on their sites; to minimise the length such content is present; and to swiftly take down this content. · However, ‘Category 2a’ firms - **search engines such as Google - have a less stringent legal duty**, and will only be required to " **minimise the risk"** of individuals encountering scams content on their site. Many users will struggle to differentiate between different types of platforms and between legitimate and illegitimate content. · This raises concerns about whether the legal duty for search engines is stringent enough and would **create a loophole in protections that scammers could exploit,** with potentially more fraud perpetrated via paid-for advertising on search engines. · **We recommend amendment 1.1 in the annex to address this.** 2.**The Bill’s measures will not apply to all online advertising providers.** · Firms defined by the government as ‘ **Category 2b’** - which are smaller, ‘user-to-user’ websites that host adverts - have not been included in the scope of the Bill.
chunks.display_text: safest place in the world to be online", then **Government must apply the measures in the Bill to paid-for advertisements**. As the Draft Bill Committee’s report makes clear: *excluding paid-for advertising will leave service providers with little incentive to remove harmful adverts, and risks encouraging further proliferation of such content.* The announcement earlier this year signalling this change was welcomed across Parliament and industry. However, we have concerns about potential gaps in the Bill’s provisions for tackling scams which criminals will seek to exploit. 1.**The legal requirement for search engines to tackle scam adverts is weaker and seems less onerous than for social media platforms.** · The Bill stipulates that firms defined as ‘Category 1’ - which are **large user-to-user sites such as Twitter and Facebook** - will have a duty to prevent people from encountering scam content on their sites; to minimise the length such content is present; and to swiftly take down this content. · However, ‘Category 2a’ firms - **search engines such as Google - have a less stringent legal duty**, and will only be required to " **minimise the risk"** of individuals encountering scams content on their site. Many users will struggle to differentiate between different types of platforms and between legitimate and illegitimate content. · This raises concerns about whether the legal duty for search engines is stringent enough and would **create a loophole in protections that scammers could exploit,** with potentially more fraud perpetrated via paid-for advertising on search engines. · **We recommend amendment 1.1 in the annex to address this.** 2.**The Bill’s measures will not apply to all online advertising providers.** · Firms defined by the government as ‘ **Category 2b’** - which are smaller, ‘user-to-user’ websites that host adverts - have not been included in the scope of the Bill.

chunks.text: recommend amendment 1.1 in the annex to address this.** 2.**The Bill’s measures will not apply to all online advertising providers.** · Firms defined by the government as ‘ **Category 2b’** - which are smaller, ‘user-to-user’ websites that host adverts - have not been included in the scope of the Bill. · It is not clear yet which specific websites will be categorised as ‘2b’. But by leaving them outside of the scope of the Bill, there is a risk that scammers will target consumers through paid-for content on these sites. · While we would expect the **Online Advertising Programme (OAP) to plug some of the gaps left by the Bill, including by covering the full range of online advertising providers, there is a lack of clarity around how the OAP will protect consumers and when these protections will be introduced.** Consumers will be given more security if Category 2b firms are covered in the Bill. 3.**The Bill’s language is often unclear and open to interpretation** · We are concerned that **many terms in the Bill are open to interpretation, leaving the guidance it aims to offer unclear and ambiguous in some areas**. One example of such language is: o Clause 34(1), which asks providers to ‘minimise the length of time’ fraudulent advertising is present. It also asks providers to ‘swiftly take down such content’ when made aware of it. Concrete guidance should be offered instead of ambiguous requests. o There are many more such examples throughout the Bill. **Ofcom responsibilities**
chunks.display_text: recommend amendment 1.1 in the annex to address this.** 2.**The Bill’s measures will not apply to all online advertising providers.** · Firms defined by the government as ‘ **Category 2b’** - which are smaller, ‘user-to-user’ websites that host adverts - have not been included in the scope of the Bill. · It is not clear yet which specific websites will be categorised as ‘2b’. But by leaving them outside of the scope of the Bill, there is a risk that scammers will target consumers through paid-for content on these sites. · While we would expect the **Online Advertising Programme (OAP) to plug some of the gaps left by the Bill, including by covering the full range of online advertising providers, there is a lack of clarity around how the OAP will protect consumers and when these protections will be introduced.** Consumers will be given more security if Category 2b firms are covered in the Bill. 3.**The Bill’s language is often unclear and open to interpretation** · We are concerned that **many terms in the Bill are open to interpretation, leaving the guidance it aims to offer unclear and ambiguous in some areas**. One example of such language is: o Clause 34(1), which asks providers to ‘minimise the length of time’ fraudulent advertising is present. It also asks providers to ‘swiftly take down such content’ when made aware of it. Concrete guidance should be offered instead of ambiguous requests. o There are many more such examples throughout the Bill. **Ofcom responsibilities**

chunks.text: 34(1), which asks providers to ‘minimise the length of time’ fraudulent advertising is present. It also asks providers to ‘swiftly take down such content’ when made aware of it. Concrete guidance should be offered instead of ambiguous requests. o There are many more such examples throughout the Bill. **Ofcom responsibilities** The ABI is supportive of the appointment of Ofcom as the regulator for online safety. It is imperative that Ofcom is equipped with adequate resources, technical expertise, the ability to request all necessary information in transparency reports (including that relating to fraudulent advertising) and a full suite of enforcement powers in order to regulate effectively. We welcome, for example, the new power to hold senior managers criminally liable if they do not comply with Ofcom information requests in an accurate and timely manner two months after the Act is introduced, rather than the two years previously mooted. It is equally important that the Bill does not inadvertently undermine the protections offered by other existing laws. T he Bill should not affect other legislation that imposes more extensive duties or liabilities on platforms. Ofcom should also strive to develop networks that enable it to engage and cooperate effectively with other regulators with online expertise so that there is a fully joined-up approach to regulation of the digital economy. **Annex: proposed amendment** **Amendment 1.1:** Different obligations for search and social platforms
chunks.display_text: 34(1), which asks providers to ‘minimise the length of time’ fraudulent advertising is present. It also asks providers to ‘swiftly take down such content’ when made aware of it. Concrete guidance should be offered instead of ambiguous requests. o There are many more such examples throughout the Bill. **Ofcom responsibilities** The ABI is supportive of the appointment of Ofcom as the regulator for online safety. It is imperative that Ofcom is equipped with adequate resources, technical expertise, the ability to request all necessary information in transparency reports (including that relating to fraudulent advertising) and a full suite of enforcement powers in order to regulate effectively. We welcome, for example, the new power to hold senior managers criminally liable if they do not comply with Ofcom information requests in an accurate and timely manner two months after the Act is introduced, rather than the two years previously mooted. It is equally important that the Bill does not inadvertently undermine the protections offered by other existing laws. T he Bill should not affect other legislation that imposes more extensive duties or liabilities on platforms. Ofcom should also strive to develop networks that enable it to engage and cooperate effectively with other regulators with online expertise so that there is a fully joined-up approach to regulation of the digital economy. **Annex: proposed amendment** **Amendment 1.1:** Different obligations for search and social platforms

chunks.text:
Page 34, line 3, after "to" leave out to end of line 5 and insert-

(a) prevent individuals fro m encountering content consisting of fraudulent advertisements

in or via search results of the service;

(b) minimise the length of time for which any such content consisting of fraudulent

advertisements is accessible in or via search results of the service;

(c) where the provider is alerted by a person to the presence of such content, or becomes

aware of it in any other way, swiftly take down such content."

Page 34, line 31, after "has" insert "or may reasonably be expected to have"

*May 2022*

---

\[1\] Action Fraud, Nov 2020, https://www.actionfraud.police.uk/news/action-fraud-warns-of-rise-in-investment-fraud-reports-as-nation-enters-second-lockdown
